steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Transitions question. The first sentence establishes that a poll showed Kennedy lost the 1960 debate badly — only 21% of radio listeners rated him the winner. The second sentence states the debate was ultimately considered a victory for Kennedy among TV viewers. These two ideas directly contradict each other, so the blank needs a contrast transition.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'In other words,': 'In other words' signals a restatement or clarification of the previous idea. The second sentence doesn't restate the first — it contradicts it. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Therefore,': 'Therefore' signals a conclusion or consequence that follows logically from what came before. Kennedy being considered the winner doesn't follow from him losing the radio poll — it contradicts it. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Likewise,': 'Likewise' signals similarity or continuation. The two outcomes are opposite, not similar. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Nevertheless,': 'Nevertheless' means 'despite what was just said, the opposite holds.' This fits perfectly: despite the radio poll showing Kennedy lost badly, he was nevertheless ultimately considered the winner by TV viewers. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
